feed.title: SAP Community - ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model

entry.title: How to create a Custom Business Configurations app that used the strict mode 2
entry.content:
Introduction

Recently we got a customer influence request with the ask to release the RAP transport API’s so that the same can be used in RAP business objects that use the strict mode 2.

An example of such a RAP business object is a Business Configuration Maintenance Object that has been generated using the ABAP Repository Object Generator.

When you just change the behavior definition and the behavior projection such that they use the strict mode 2 instead of the basic strict mode 

 

 

 

managed with additional save implementation in class ZBP_I_HOLIDAYCAL2_S unique;
//strict;
strict ( 2 );
with draft;

 

 

 

and

 

 

 

projection implementation in class ZBP_C_HOLIDAYCAL2_S unique;
//strict;
strict ( 2 );
use draft;

 

 

 

we would get the following dump when trying to save the data to a customizing request:

Statement "EXEC SQL COMMIT" is not allowed with this status.

Since making the existing RAP transport API's compatible with the strict mode 2 is probably a task that cannot be achieved at all together with colleagues I thought about possible workarounds.

We came up with the idea to wrap the calls of the transport API's which cause the trouble using the background processing framework.

This can be achieved by some small changes to the save_modified method of the behavior implementation class and by providing a wrapper class that implements the interfaces of the background processing framework.

So let’s get started.

Generate a business configuration maintenance object

For this blog post I have used the tables listed at the end of the blog post and the generator for business configuration maintenance objects which is described in this tutorial

Create Business Configuration Maintenance Object | SAP Tutorials

In addition one has to create appropriate authorizations for the CDS view that will be generated (here ZI_HolidayCal2)  rah

 

 

 

Changes in the behavior implementation class

In the behavior implementation class we have to adapt the save_modified( ) method and we have to add types definitions in the global behavior class which can be used by our wrapper class.

Local saver class

In the save_modified method we comment out the call of the local class lhc_rap_tdat_cts which contains the call of the RAP transport API.

Instead we are using a class called zcl_record_changes_via_bgpf which implements the interfaces if_serializable_object, if_bgmc_operation and if_bgmc_op_single_tx_uncontr.

The latter allows us to call API’s that may contain commit work statements which would cause a dump when being called directly in the behavior implementation. Instead this class acts as wrapper and and starts the call of the transport API asynchronously in a new process.

 

 

CLASS lsc_zi_holidaycal2_s DEFINITION FINAL INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_saver.
  PROTECTED SECTION.
    METHODS:
      save_modified REDEFINITION,
      cleanup_finalize REDEFINITION.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS lsc_zi_holidaycal2_s IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD save_modified.

    READ TABLE update-HolidayCal2All INDEX 1 INTO DATA(all).
    IF all-TransportRequestID IS NOT INITIAL.

      "start new implementation

      DATA bgpf_operation TYPE REF TO zcl_record_changes_via_bgpf .

      bgpf_operation = NEW zcl_record_changes_via_bgpf(
        i_transport_request = all-TransportRequestID
        i_create            = create
        i_update            = update
        i_delete            = delete
      ).
      TRY.
          DATA(background_process) = cl_bgmc_process_factory=>get_default(  )->create(  ).
          background_process->set_operation_tx_uncontrolled( bgpf_operation ).
          background_process->set_name( CONV #( all-TransportRequestID ) ).
          background_process->save_for_execution(  ).
        CATCH cx_bgmc.
          "handle exception
          ASSERT 1 = 2.
      ENDTRY.

      "end new implementation

*      lhc_rap_tdat_cts=>get( )->record_changes(
*                                  transport_request = all-TransportRequestID
*                                  create            = REF #( create )
*                                  update            = REF #( update )
*                                  delete            = REF #( delete ) ).

    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.
  METHOD cleanup_finalize ##NEEDED.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

 

 

 

Global class

In the global class we add three type statements so that we can reuse the same in the constructor of our wrapper class.

 

 

CLASS zbp_i_holidaycal2_s DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  ABSTRACT
  FINAL
  FOR BEHAVIOR OF zi_holidaycal2_s .

  PUBLIC SECTION.

    TYPES : t_create TYPE REQUEST FOR CHANGE zi_holidaycal2_s,
            t_update TYPE REQUEST FOR CHANGE zi_holidaycal2_s,
            t_delete type request for delete zi_holidaycal2_s .
  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zbp_i_holidaycal2_s IMPLEMENTATION.
ENDCLASS.

 

 

 

Wrapper class zcl_record_changes_via_bgpf

The wrapper class contains fairly generic coding. The constructor takes the three complex structures which contains the data which shall be written to our customizing request.

Since the class will be serialized by the background processing framework I have made sure that the constructor does not take the references but the actual values of the tables that are passed by the behavior implementation.

 

 

 

CLASS zcl_record_changes_via_bgpf DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.

    INTERFACES if_serializable_object .
    INTERFACES if_bgmc_operation .
    INTERFACES if_bgmc_op_single_tx_uncontr .




    METHODS constructor
      IMPORTING i_transport_request TYPE sxco_transport
                i_create            TYPE zbp_i_holidaycal2_s=>t_create
                i_update            TYPE zbp_i_holidaycal2_s=>t_update
                i_delete            TYPE zbp_i_holidaycal2_s=>t_delete.


  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.

    DATA : transport_request TYPE sxco_transport,
           create            TYPE zbp_i_holidaycal2_s=>t_create,
           update            TYPE zbp_i_holidaycal2_s=>t_update,
           delete            TYPE zbp_i_holidaycal2_s=>t_delete.
ENDCLASS.



CLASS zcl_record_changes_via_bgpf IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD constructor.
    create = i_create.
    update = i_update.
    delete = i_delete.
    transport_request = i_transport_request.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD if_bgmc_op_single_tx_uncontr~execute.

    lhc_rap_tdat_cts=>get( )->record_changes(
                                transport_request = transport_request
                                create            = REF #( create )
                                update            = REF #( update )
                                delete            = REF #( delete ) ).

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

 

 

Local class LHC_RAP_TDAT_CTS

Our wrapper class zcl_record_changes_via_bgpf uses the same code as the one that we have commented out in the save_modified( ) method of the generated behavior implementation.

We have thus to create a local class LHC_RAP_TDAT_CTS in our global class zcl_record_changes_via_bgpf as well.

 

 

CLASS LHC_RAP_TDAT_CTS DEFINITION FINAL.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    CLASS-METHODS:
      GET
        RETURNING
          VALUE(RESULT) TYPE REF TO IF_MBC_CP_RAP_TDAT_CTS.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS LHC_RAP_TDAT_CTS IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD GET.
    result = mbc_cp_api=>rap_tdat_cts( tdat_name = 'ZHOLIDAYCAL2'
                                       table_entity_relations = VALUE #(
                                         ( entity = 'HolidayCal2' table = 'ZBC_FCAL_HOLI' )
                                         ( entity = 'HolidayCal2Text' table = 'ZBC_FCAL_HOLI_T' )
                                       ) ) ##NO_TEXT.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

 

 

Result

When we now start the business configuration app and enter data and save the same,

this data can be written to customizing request that we have to create beforehand.

 

Tables used

For this demo I used the following tables and generated a business configuration maintenance object based on these two tables

zbc_fcal_holi

 

@EndUserText.label : 'Holiday Cal2'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #C
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #ALLOWED
define table zbc_fcal_holi {

  key client            : mandt not null;
  key holiday_id        : zdmo_fcal_holiday_id not null;
  month_of_holiday      : fdt_month;
  day_of_holiday        : fdt_day_of_month;
  configdeprecationcode : config_deprecation_code;
  last_changed_at       : abp_lastchange_tstmpl;
  local_last_changed_at : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl;

}

 

zbc_fcal_holi_t

 

@EndUserText.label : 'Holiday Cal2 Text Table'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #C
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #ALLOWED
define table zbc_fcal_holi_t {

  key client            : mandt not null;
  @AbapCatalog.textLanguage
  key lang              : abap.lang not null;
  @AbapCatalog.foreignKey.keyType : #TEXT_KEY
  @AbapCatalog.foreignKey.screenCheck : false
  key holiday_id        : zdmo_fcal_holiday_id not null
    with foreign key [0..*,1] zbc_fcal_holi
      where client = zbc_fcal_holi_t.client
        and holiday_id = zbc_fcal_holi_t.holiday_id;
  fcal_description      : sxco_ao_short_description;
  local_last_changed_at : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl;

}

entry.title: What Is Entity Manipulation Language and How to use EML operation during CRUD
entry.content:
In This Blog We Will Discussed About What Is EML Operation In RAP And How To Create a EML Operation.  

EML stands for Entity manipulating language. 

EML is a part of ABAP and using which user can access the data of RAP business objects in an ABAP Program. 

EML statements allow the data content of a RAP Business object to be read and modified. 

It is a part of ABAP to manage to read, create, update, delete the data into the DB Table 

In which version EML is available?  

EML - Entity Manipulation Language is available after 1902 Cloud release and 1909 on-premise release.

EML Flow 

 

Generally where is EML used ?  

Usually, Business objects that are implemented with the ABAP RESTful architecture based on the behavior definition and implementation of the interaction phase and save sequence in behavior pools can be consumed by means of OData protocol (Fiori UIs, or Web APIs). 

Now to access them through ABAP code EML syntax is used. 

First we can check Flow of EML operation With Fun analogy: -  

  1. Birthday party house  
  2. Cake preparing Shop Baker will preparing a cake 
  3. Once that person prepares cake, he needs to give it to Delivery 
  4. Delivery boy will send that cake to Birthday boy House 
  5. Once birthday boy received a cake then they will celebrate a birthday 

For this flow we can do some modification. 

ROOT ENTITY, EML, SAVE SEQUENCE, FINAL DATA, DATABASE, how these 5 components interact with our ABAP Rap. 

First baker man will prepare a cake whatever they need to add it in cake like Cherry, Cream, once he prepares a cake baker man will Give it to Delivery person. 

Here baker man Indicates EML 

Once The delivery person will deliver the cake to the  Birthday party house, House will be representing the database, and final data will be representing the Cake.

Final data means cake will be entered to the DB table that is Birthday Boy. 

And a few more concepts we can analogy with this concept The SAVE SEQUENCE can take a multiple Routes. 

Once cake is delivered to home Then we can celebrate a birthday. 

Save sequence can have multiple Routes Save such as, 

  • Un-managed save ---> Customization of SQL Query or BAPI CALL FUNCTION MODULE to perform save the data to the DATABASE. 
  • Save with additional save --> Apart from the standard save, internally or runtime custom code will run before the final save is called. 
  • Save with modify --> complete overriding code with a existing save at runtime and completely modifying in managed scenario itself. 

By this fun analogy we can get to know, When the commit is triggered then only, we can do the crud operation to the Database table Using EML concept. 

COMMIT ENTITIES: -  

Commit entities Triggered ABAP RAP framework When save sequence RAP Business object is changed using Modify statement is saved in Transactional buffer, the saving of changed or created data to the DATABASE must be triggered which is done by COMMIT ENTITIES. 

Now let us see what are steps we need to follow while creating a EML operation In RAP: -  

Steps to create EML 

  1. Create a Database table For required fields 
  2. Create Interface CDS view  
  3. Create Consumption CDS view 
  4. Add metadata extension to the consumption view 
  5. Create Behavior definition 
  6. Create a service definition 
  7. Create a service binding 
  8. Create a  Behavior implementation  

1. Create a database table with the name of (ZJBM_DT_USER)

2. Create Interface CDS view (ZI_JBM_USER) 

3. Created a Consumption CDS view and also add @Metadata.allowExtensions: true Annotation (ZC_JBM_USER) 

4. Create a metadata extension to the consumption view (ZC_JBM_USER) 

5. Create a behavior definition of consumption view 

6. Now create a service definition 

 7. Create a service binding 

8. Select a consumption view and click on preview, we can see field in Front end And we can see there is no data is Present 

Now we need to Create a data with the help of EML operation 

For behavior definition we need to create a behavior implementation after we need to Do crud operation.  In behavior implementation write a crud operation. 

For this purpose, am taking instance method like SAMPLE_CREATE, SAMPLE_READ,  SAMPLE_UPDATE, SAMPLE_DELETE. 

 First am starting with SAMPLE_CREATE method implementation. 

We need to declare an interface as IF_OO_ADT_CLASSRUN  

This interface is used to do CRUD operation in Business object. 

First am going to implement Sample_create method. 

Declaring the internal table for root entity view 

%CONTROL this will tells like which are the fields you want to store to the DB table 

IF_ABAP_BEHV this interface helps to insert the data to the DB table,  

If you don’t want to insert the data for the particular fields we can make it as  

IF_ABAP_ BEHV=>MK-OFF 

Here am  inserting the data to the Database so am using IF_ABAP_BEHV=>MK-ON. 

Modify entities this statement will saves the data to the Transactional Buffer. 

After am taking MAPPED DATA, FAILED DATA, REPORTED DATA now i will explain what are those. 

Mapped data: - contains key mapping information (contains table key value) only available for modify entity or modify entities). 

Failed data: - Contains details about operation which is not successful or that cloud not be processed. 

Reported data: -  contains any error message as result of failed operation  

Inside a interface method we need to call the sample_create method. After method we need to use COMMIT_ENTITIES Statement once the commit entity statement is triggered then only data will be updated into the data base table. 

Now execute the code in ABAP CONSOLE 

Now we can see the database table Updated record 

 

Now I will do SAMPLE_UPDATE method to update the data. 

Here we need to mention which fields we are going to update in DB table 

Inside a Interface method calling the SAMPLE_UPDATE method and Commit entities Statement 

In Database table we can see the changes in Updated data  

 

Now we can go for SAMPLE_READ method Implementation. 

This logic inside a interface method 

Output in console: 

Now am doing SAMPLE_DELETE method implementation. 

Inside a SAMPLE_DELETE method logic 

Calling SAMPLE_DELETE method in a interface  

Output: - 

Refer the code below for the whole program inside a behavior implementation class.   

 

 

CLASS zbp_c_jbm_user DEFINITION PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PUBLIC . 
PUBLIC SECTION. 
INTERFACES : if_oo_adt_classrun. 
METHODS : sample_read, 
sample_update, 
sample_delete, 
sample_create. 
ENDCLASS. 

CLASS zbp_c_jbm_user IMPLEMENTATION. 
METHOD sample_create. 
DATA : lt_user TYPE TABLE FOR CREATE zc_jbm_user. 
lt_user = VALUE #( ( userid = 2 
userName = 'Jyothi' 
userage = 22 
%control = VALUE #( 
userid = if_abap_behv=>mk-on 
username = if_abap_behv=>mk-on 
userage = if_abap_behv=>mk-on ) 
) ) . 
MODIFY ENTITIES OF zc_jbm_user ENTITY user CREATE FROM lt_user 
MAPPED DATA(lt_mapped) 
FAILED DATA(lt_failed) 
REPORTED DATA(Lt_reported). 
ENDMETHOD. 

METHOD sample_update. 
MODIFY ENTITY zc_jbm_user 
UPDATE FIELDS ( Username UserAge ) 
WITH VALUE #( ( 
UserId = 1 
UserName = 'Swathi' 
UserAge = 28 ) ). 
ENDMETHOD. 

METHOD sample_read. 
* READ ENTITY zc_jbm_user ALL FIELDS WITH VALUE #( ( 
* UserId = 1 ) ) 
* RESULT DATA(lt_read_data). 
ENDMETHOD. 

METHOD sample_delete. 
MODIFY ENTITY zc_jbm_user 
DELETE FROM VALUE #( ( 
UserId = 2 ) ). 
ENDMETHOD.

METHOD if_oo_adt_classrun~main. 
"===========Sample create method calling inside a interface Main 
sample_create( ). 
COMMIT ENTITIES. 
IF sy-subrc = 0. 
WRITE : / 'Data Is Inseted In Database Table'. 
ELSE. 
WRITE : / 'No data Inserted'. 
ENDIF. 
"============Sample update method=========== 
sample_update( ). 
COMMIT ENTITIES. 
IF sy-subcs = 0 . 
WRITE : / 'Record update to The DB table'. 
ELSE. 
WRITE : / 'No Record update to the DB table'. 
ENDIF. 
"=====================sample_read method======= 
READ ENTITY zc_jbm_user ALL FIELDS WITH 
VALUE #( ( 
UserId = 1 ) ) 
RESULT DATA(lt_result). 
out->write( lt_result ). 

" ================Sample delete method 
sample_delete( ). 
COMMIT ENTITIES. 
IF sy-subcs = 0 . 
WRITE : / 'Record Deleted to The DB table'. 
ELSE. 
WRITE : / 'No Record Deleted to the DB table'. 
ENDIF. 
ENDMETHOD. 
ENDCLASS.

 

 

 

Without using behavior implementation how can we use EML in Report program 

Inside a SAMPLE_CREATE METHOD Logic 

Create a object in a report level. 

In CDS table we can see the inserted DATA 

Now we can implement the sample_update method. 

We can see the Updated record in Table 

Now we can do for SAMPLE_DELETE Implementation 

In report we need to call those method and use it 

Output: -

entry.title: SAP Developer News, August 29th, 2024
entry.content:
Podcast: https://podcast.opensap.info/sap-developers/2024/08/29/sap-developer-news-august-29th-2024/

DESCRIPTION

SAP BTP ABAP Environment Release 2408

  • Release Notes Blog Post: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/sap-btp-abap-environment-release-2408/ba-p/13801154

SAP TechEd Registration

  • SAP TechEd: https://www.sap.com/events/teched.html
  • SAP TechEd Session Catalog: https://www.sap.com/events/teched/virtual/flow/sap/te24/catalog/page/catalog

New CodeJam: Getting started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core

  • New CodeJam in the topic list: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-codejam-blog-posts/sap-codejam-topics/ba-p/221407
  • CodeJam event in Warsaw, Poland: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-codejam/getting-started-with-generative-ai-hub-on-sap-ai-core/ec-p/13809704#M572

ABAP Developer Challenge and Devtoberfest

  • ABAP Developer Challenge: https://community.sap.com/t5/application-development-blog-posts/sap-developer-challenge-abap-core-data-services/ba-p/13778288
  • Devtoberfest: https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest-blog-posts/devtoberfest-2024-contest/ba-p/13781593

CHAPTER TITLES

0:00 Intro

0:10 SAP BTP ABAP Environment Release 2408

2:15 SAP TechEd Registration

3:08 New CodeJam: Getting started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core

4:04 ABAP Developer Challenge and Devtoberfest

Transcript

[Rich] Hey folks, Rich Heilman here, and yes it is time for yet again another release of the BTP ABAP environment, release 2408. And once again, this release is jam packed with new features for ABAP developers. Just to name a few, ABAP Developer Tools now supports a read-only fallback editor for development objects that do not have a native editor, for example BSPs, and also ADT now supports displaying warning messages coming from the HANA database while debugging nested AMDPs. CDS now supports external databases via ABAP CDS and ABAP SQL using CDS external entities. We also have support for logical external schemas for outbound SQL access with external entities. There has been updates to the ABAP repository object wizard, which now includes generating ABAP CDS provider models. There is also new support for creating CDS aspects to store and reuse field definitions and calculations. and we also have support for a few new annotations as well.

There are a bunch of new features around UI services in Fiori Launchpad, like support for adding a custom logo to the Launchpad and other customizing of the browser window. A lot of new features around integration topics like direct push-based integration over RFC, new enhancements around the advanced event mesh, and creating and assigning API packages.

Now I've only touched on a few of the new features to hit release 24.08. So, if you want the full story and all of the details, be sure to check out the 24.08 release blog post today. See you next time.

[Josh] It's time for SAP TechEd. Every year, TechEd is our time to get together, share ideas, learn, and have a lot of fun. Last year's TechEd was awesome, and good news, registration for TechEd 2024 is officially open. We've got an incredible TechEd lineup. First off, two full days of keynote analysis, code demos over 80 live sessions, three channels of programming, and live Q&A. That's right, they're still letting me talk live on a microphone. Then, and I am so excited about this, join us on tour as we make our way around the world with in-person stops on three continents throughout 2024 and 2025. We can't wait to connect in person for deep dive sessions, hands-on labs, and continue learning together. And trust me, this is a TechEd you will not wanna miss. See you online in October.

[Nora] Hey there, we have a new Generative AI CodeJam on the list. The first two events are already scheduled. One of them will be on the 20th of September in Warsaw, Poland at the KMD office and the other one will be right after Virtual TechEd in Munich. During the CodeJam you will learn how to access different large range models via Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core. We will use the Python SDK of Generative AI Hub and we will also use the SAP AI Launchpad to access the models. You will also learn how to implement a retrieval augmented generation use case using the HANA Vector Engine and we will have a look at different chunking techniques, how you can chunk your documents right and then we will also have a look at a multi modal model where you will learn how to work with images and text. So, make sure to also check out all the other great code gems on the list, find an event near you or host your own event. See you there! Hello, SAP developers.

[Shilpa] I hope you are enjoying the August Developer Challenge on ABAP Core Data Services. The last task in this four-week challenge was out yesterday. We have seen huge response over 550 plus submissions throughout the challenge and thanks to each one of you for taking time and as well as involving in some good discussions on best practices. For those of you who have not yet taken part in the challenge, you have one more week left until we close it. In this four-week task, you will learn about the basics of ABAP series, annotations, associations, criticality and object page. While this challenge is coming to an end, we have another exciting event kicking off in September and that is Devtoberfest. We are back again with lots of interesting sessions which ranges from September 23rd until October 25th. Like last year, we have dedicated topics each day ranging from ABAP, CAP, SAP Build, Build Code, integration, machine learning, AI, and front-end. Most of the sessions are already scheduled in the community event page. Check it out and happy learning!

entry.title: Side Effects in Fiori: Triggering Actions and Updating Data in Your Unmanaged RAP Application
entry.content:
Understanding Side Effects

Side Effects refer to system behaviors where a user’s action, such as modifying a field or performing a specific task, triggers changes in other parts of the user interface. For instance, updating a field might automatically adjust related fields or data sections. These changes are crucial for maintaining data consistency and ensuring that the user interface reflects the latest information.

User ActionSide Effect
Triggering an ActionAfter executing an action successfully, the collection for which the action is defined is refreshed automatically if the following conditions apply:
1. The action is a bound action.
2. The returned instance does not correspond to the bound instance. For example, copy actions.

In scenarios where side effects are not natively supported by the RAP layer, we can achieve similar functionality through Fiori annotations. Specifically, side effects can be implemented through action triggers and subsequent data refreshes in the Fiori layer.

Scenario Overview: Redetermine All Quotations

Consider a scenario where you have a button labeled "Redetermine All Quotations". Upon clicking this button, multiple columns in the item table are updated. For the purpose of this demonstration, we will focus on two key columns that are updated: Ref. Quotation and Quotation Items. The goal is to ensure that these updates are reflected immediately in the Fiori application without requiring manual refreshes.

 

Implementing Side Effects with Fiori Annotations

To address the requirement of real-time data updates, follow these steps:

  1. Open the Fiori Project in BAS: Navigate to the annotation.xml file within your Fiori project. This file initially displays the EDMX (Entity Data Model XML) structure.

  2. Add the Required Annotations: Insert the following code snippet into the annotation.xml file to configure side effects for your application. This annotation will ensure that the relevant data is refreshed automatically after executing an action.

     


    <edmx:Edmx xmlns:edmx="http://docs.oasis-open.org/odata/ns/edmx" Version="4.0">
    <edmx:Reference Uri="https://sap.github.io/odata-vocabularies/vocabularies/Common.xml">
    <edmx:Include Namespace="com.sap.vocabularies.Common.v1" Alias="Common"/>
    </edmx:Reference>
    <edmx:Reference Uri="https://sap.github.io/odata-vocabularies/vocabularies/UI.xml">
    <edmx:Include Namespace="com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1" Alias="UI"/>
    </edmx:Reference>
    <edmx:Reference Uri="https://sap.github.io/odata-vocabularies/vocabularies/Communication.xml">
    <edmx:Include Namespace="com.sap.vocabularies.Communication.v1" Alias="Communication"/>
    </edmx:Reference>
    <edmx:Reference Uri="/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZUI_DRAFT_SALES_ORDERS/$metadata">
    <edmx:Include Namespace="cds_zui_draft_orders"/>
    </edmx:Reference>
    <edmx:DataServices>
    <Schema xmlns="http://docs.oasis-open.org/odata/ns/edm" Namespace="local">
    <Annotations Target="cds_zui_draft_orders.cds_zui_draft_orders_Entities/Refresh_items">
    <Annotation Term="Common.SideEffects">
    <Record Type="Common.SideEffectsType">
    <PropertyValue Property="TargetEntities">
    <Collection>
    <NavigationPropertyPath>_it/to_items</NavigationPropertyPath>
    </Collection>
    </PropertyValue>
    </Record>
    </Annotation>
    </Annotations>
    </Schema>
    </edmx:DataServices>
    </edmx:Edmx>

  3. Test the Implementation: After updating the annotation file, return to the Fiori application. Open the developer tools and navigate to the network tab. Click the "Redetermine All Quotations" button, which triggers the action with the technical name refresh_items. You will observe a POST call for the action and a subsequent GET call to refresh the entire entity set of items as defined in the annotation.

     

  4. Verify Data Update: Check the data section of the items. You should see the updated values reflected immediately without the need for a manual refresh.

    Conclusion

    By leveraging Fiori annotations to implement side effects and action triggers, you can ensure that your unmanaged RAP application remains responsive and up-to-date. This approach effectively addresses the limitations of the RAP layer and enhances the overall user experience by maintaining data consistency in real-time.
    Feel free to share your comments and feedback. This approach is designed to keep your data up-to-date in real-time, ensuring a smooth and dynamic experience for your users.

entry.title: How to display ALV GRID with only structure
RAP additional save
entry.content:
Managed RAP additional save.

Introduction.

We can add external functionality to the managed save sequence by using the extra save. This feature facilitates the reusing of services such as change documents and application logs. The RAP framework defaults to handling managed saves in this way; however, additional steps can be added to the managed save. Here, we use the saver class and the save_modfied method. Updated fields, updated data, and completed operations are all saved when an operation is carried out on an entity's instance. In this instance, the log table will contain any changed data that takes place within an entity instance.

 In this scenario for the data modified in the instance of an entity the changed data will be saved in the log table.

: create the log table to save the data. 

 

 

 

 

@EndUserText.label : 'log data changes table' 

@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE 

@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT 

@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A 

@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED 

define table znp_t_log_travel { 

 

key client : abap.clnt not null; 

key change_id : abap.raw(16) not null; 

travelid : /dmo/travel_id not null; 

changing_operation : abap.char(10); 

changed_field_name : abap.char(32); 

changed_value : abap.char(32); 

created_at : timestampl; 

 

}

 

 

 

 

 

 

To do this additional save  is used, to save the data in a managed scenario addition.

  • In the Behavior definition define additional save  
  • For the entity. 
  • It will create one local class and method save modified. In the implementation class. 

 

 
When a new instance is created, the additional data is saved in the entity's database. Log table. 

For to this implement define the saver class and implement the methods in the behavior pool. 

For to save the implemented the save_modified method in the save class. 

 

 

 

 

 

 

METHOD save_modified. 
DATA : lt_travel_log TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF znp_t_log_travel. 
DATA : lt_travel_log_c TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF znp_t_log_travel. 
DATA : lt_travel_log_u TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF znp_t_log_travel. 

 

IF create-znp_i_travel_m IS NOT INITIAL. 
lt_travel_log = CORRESPONDING #( create-znp_i_travel_m ). 

LOOP AT lt_travel_log ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<ls_travel_log>). 
<ls_travel_log>-changing_operation = 'CREATE'. 
GET TIME STAMP FIELD <ls_travel_log>-created_at. 
READ TABLE create-znp_i_travel_m ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<ls_travel>) 
WITH TABLE KEY entity 
COMPONENTS TravelId = <ls_travel_log>-travelid. 

IF sy-subrc IS INITIAL. 

IF <ls_travel>-%control-BookingFee = cl_abap_behv=>flag_changed. 

<ls_travel_log>-changed_field_name = 'Booking Fee'. 

<ls_travel_log>-changed_value = <ls_travel>-BookingFee. 

TRY. 

<ls_travel_log>-change_id = cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_x16_static( ). 

CATCH cx_uuid_error. 

"handle exception 

ENDTRY. 

APPEND <ls_travel_log> TO lt_travel_log_c. 
ENDIF. 

IF <ls_travel>-%control-OverallStatus = cl_abap_behv=>flag_changed. 

<ls_travel_log>-changed_field_name = 'Overall Status'. 

<ls_travel_log>-changed_value = <ls_travel>-OverallStatus. 

TRY. 

<ls_travel_log>-change_id = cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_x16_static( ). 

CATCH cx_uuid_error. 

"handle exception 

ENDTRY. 
APPEND <ls_travel_log> TO lt_travel_log_c. 
ENDIF. 
ENDIF. 
ENDLOOP. 
INSERT znp_t_log_travel FROM TABLE @LT_travel_log_c. 
ENDIF. 

 

 

IF update-znp_i_travel_m IS NOT INITIAL. 
lt_travel_log = CORRESPONDING #( update-znp_i_travel_m ). 

LOOP AT update-znp_i_travel_m ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<ls_log_update>). 
ASSIGN lt_travel_log[ travelid = <ls_log_update>-travelid ] TO FIELD-SYMBOL(<ls_log_u>). 
<ls_log_u>-changing_operation = 'UPDATE'. 

GET TIME STAMP FIELD <ls_log_u>-created_at. 
IF <ls_log_update>-%control-customerid = if_abap_behv=>mk-on. 
<ls_log_u>-changed_value = <ls_log_update>-customerid. 

TRY. 
<ls_log_u>-change_id = cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_x16_static( ) . 
CATCH cx_uuid_error. 
ENDTRY. 

<ls_log_u>-changed_field_name = 'customer_id'. 
APPEND <ls_log_u> TO lt_travel_log_u. 
ENDIF. 

IF <ls_log_update>-%control-description = if_abap_behv=>mk-on. 
<ls_log_u>-changed_value = <ls_log_update>-description. 
TRY. 
<ls_log_u>-change_id = cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_x16_static( ) . 
CATCH cx_uuid_error. 
ENDTRY. 
<ls_log_u>-changed_field_name = 'description'. 
APPEND <ls_log_u> TO lt_travel_log_u. 
ENDIF. 
ENDLOOP. 

INSERT znp_t_log_travel FROM TABLE @LT_travel_log_u. 

ENDIF. 

IF delete-znp_i_travel_m IS NOT INITIAL. 
lt_travel_log = CORRESPONDING #( delete-znp_i_travel_m ). 
LOOP AT lt_travel_log ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<ls_log_del>). 
<ls_log_del>-changing_operation = 'DELETE'. 
GET TIME STAMP FIELD <ls_log_del>-created_at. 
TRY. 
<ls_log_del>-change_id = cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_x16_static( ) . 
CATCH cx_uuid_error. 

"handle exception 
ENDTRY. 
ENDLOOP. 

INSERT znp_t_log_travel FROM TABLE @LT_travel_log. 
ENDIF. 

 

********************************************************************** 

********************************************************************** 

 

DATA: lt_book_suppl TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF znp_t_booksuppl. 

IF create-znp_i_booksuppl IS NOT INITIAL. 
lt_book_suppl = VALUE #( FOR ls_booksup IN create-znp_i_booksuppl ( 
travel_id = ls_booksup-TravelId 
booking_id = ls_booksup-BookingId 
booking_supplement_id = ls_booksup-BookingSupplementId 
supplement_id = ls_booksup-SupplementId 
price = ls_booksup-Price 
currency_code = ls_booksup-CurrencyCode 
last_changed_at = ls_booksup-LastChangedAt 

) ). 
INSERT znp_t_booksuppl FROM TABLE @LT_book_suppl. 
endif. 

IF update-znp_i_booksuppl IS NOT INITIAL. 
lt_book_suppl = VALUE #( FOR ls_booksup IN update-znp_i_booksuppl ( 
travel_id = ls_booksup-TravelId 
booking_id = ls_booksup-BookingId 
booking_supplement_id = ls_booksup-BookingSupplementId 
supplement_id = ls_booksup-SupplementId 
price = ls_booksup-Price 
currency_code = ls_booksup-CurrencyCode 
last_changed_at = ls_booksup-LastChangedAt 

) ). 
UPDATE znp_t_booksuppl FROM TABLE @LT_book_suppl. 
ENDIF. 

IF delete-znp_i_booksuppl IS NOT INITIAL. 
lt_book_suppl = VALUE #( FOR ls_del IN delete-znp_i_booksuppl ( 

travel_id = ls_del-TravelId 

booking_id = ls_del-BookingId 

booking_supplement_id = ls_del-BookingSupplementId 

) ). 
DELETE znp_t_booksuppl FROM TABLE @LT_book_suppl. 
ENDIF. 

 

ENDMETHOD.

 

 

 

 

. 

 

 For creating an instantiation operation. 

 

 

 

 

Edit operation. 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Delete operation,

entry.title: Customer Course GW100 (SAP Gateway – Building OData Services) Available for SAP S/4HANA 2023
entry.content:
The GW100 got a “technical update”, which means that all exercises are now running on the newest SAP S/4HANA available today. In addition, the slides now follow the newest design guidelines of SAP. So, welcome to this short blog post about the customer training GW100 (SAP Gateway – Building OData Services).

If you are more interested in a digital learning for SAP Gateway, please read my blog post:
SAP Learning Journey – Building OData Services with SAP Gateway

Introduction

Starting in 2014, this is now the eighth incarnation of how to develop OData services with SAP Gateway. The course shows all parts of SAP Gateway, which are available since SAP NetWeaver Application Server 7.0 (SAP Gateway 2.0), as well as parts added up to SAP S/4HANA 2023. The whole bandwidth of SAP Gateway covering all releases is shown.

The units in the current version are the following:

  1. SAP Gateway Overview
  2. OData Overview
  3. SAP Gateway Service Implementation
  4. SAP Gateway Service Generation
  5. SAP Gateway Service Redefinition
  6. SAP Gateway and CDS Views
  7. SAP Gateway Hub Functionalities
  8. Advanced OData Options
  9. SAP Gateway Security
  10. Further Information

If customers are interested, they can order a customer specific version of the course including only those topics suitable for their release or purpose.

System Landscape

Let’s start with the system release: SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS02. Our system landscape – we call it universal target – is used in many technology courses and offers full access for the participants from the SAP Fiori launchpad (FLP) in the browser down to SAP HANA on the SUSE Linux Enterprise server (SLES). Everything is set up following the newest guidelines of SAP so that it can really act as a template for customers.

GW100 System Landscape (Screenshot from System Setup Guide)

In addition, we offer an instance of the SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) to show the consumption of OData services. If you want to know more about this topic, check out our courses for SAP Fiori development.

Example: Business Services

As an example from the material, here a shorten excerpt about defining business services.

Business Services: OData V2 and V4 (Screenshot from GW100)

In the area of business services, a service definition exposes CDS Views as a generic service. A service binding then marks it as a special kind of service. You can create an OData V2 or V4 service for API- or UI-usage. Service definitions and bindings are created in the ADT, the generated OData services are registered in transaction /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE or /IWFND/V4_ADMIN respectively.

How to Create a Service Definition and Binding (Screenshot from GW100)

Service definitions and bindings are ABAP repository objects and a substantial part of the business services. They can only be created and edited in the ADT. In the first step, the service definition just defines the name of the service. The CDS views are added later in the source code.

In the second step, the service binding refers to the service definition and sets the binding type, which are mainly communication protocols.

Service Definition and Binding Correlation (Screenshot from GW100)

By exposing a CDS View in a service definition, the name of the entity set can be defined. For the entity type, "Type" is automatically appended to the entity set name.

The entity set names and navigation properties based on the associations in the CDS views are visible in the service binding on the right. For OData V2, multiple versions of a service definition can be added to a service binding visible on the left. For OData V4, multiple service definitions can be added to one service binding to allow cross-service navigation.


Summary

If you have read so far, it seems that you are really thinking about attending the course. Don’t hesitate and check out the physical and virtual events we offer:

https://training.sap.com/course/GW100

If you are more interested in a digital learning, please visit https://learning.sap.com. Some parts of the GW100 are already available free-of-charge:

https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/building-odata-services-with-sap-gateway

For any other questions around the offerings of SAP Learning Services, please jump to our community page and get in contact with us:

https://pages.community.sap.com/topics/training-certification

I hope even if you won’t attend the GW100 in any kind, you found some useful information around SAP Gateway in this blog post.

Happy learning
Stefan

entry.title: Pivoting to S/4HANA: A Comprehensive Guide for Veteran ABAP Developers
entry.content:
Understand the Basics of S/4HANA 

To fully embrace S/4HANA, you first need to grasp its fundamentals. Unveiled in 2015, S/4HANA leverages the power of the HANA database - SAP's in-memory database solution. With real-time analytics and applications, S/4HANA provides businesses with unprecedented performance and agility. 

SAP S/4HANA, or SAP Business Suite 4 SAP HANA, is SAP's next-generation business suite and the company's biggest innovation since SAP R/3. It is a new product line completely built on the advanced in-memory platform, SAP HANA, and offers a personalized user experience with SAP Fiori.

Here are the basic elements you, as a veteran ABAP Developer, need to understand about S/4HANA:

1. Simplified Data Model: S/4HANA simplifies the data model significantly. It eliminates redundancy, reducing the data footprint and improving transactional speed. Traditional aggregate and index tables have been removed which means the same set of data is no longer updated in multiple tables.

2. Advanced Processing: Utilising the processing power of the HANA in-memory database, S/4HANA enables advanced processing to handle massive volumes of data at high speed. This includes functionalities such as predictive analytics, spatial processing, text analytics, and data virtualization.

3. Real-time Analytics: Unlike older versions of SAP ERP, S/4HANA allows performing real-time analytics at the level of a single transaction. It can run OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) and OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) on a single system.

4. SAP Fiori UX: S/4HANA uses the SAP Fiori user interface, presenting a simplified and intuitive user experience across various devices and deployment options. It significantly enhances user productivity and satisfaction.

5. Scope and Versatility: S/4HANA can be utilized on-premise, in the cloud, or through hybrid models, offering unprecedented versatility. It spans the entire enterprise, encompassing finance, logistics, sales, and business planning among others.

6. Coding Shift: With S/4HANA, you’ll experience a shift in coding since the applications are predominantly written in ABAP but designed to take advantage of HANA's features such as parallelization and columnar storage.

7. HANA-Optimised ABAP: ABAP stays as the main language for S/4HANA, but with new functionalities optimised for SAP HANA database. This includes Database Procedure Proxy, ABAP Managed Database Procedures (AMDP), and Core Data Services (CDS).

Transitioning to S/4HANA calls for a paradigm shift in the way you approach ABAP programming. However, with its simplified architecture and advanced capabilities, learning and adapting to S/4HANA paves the way for great opportunities ahead.

 

Dive into HANA Database 

The backbone of S/4HANA is the HANA database. As an ABAP developer, understanding the internal mechanism of HANA database can provide you a significant edge. The HANA database is column-oriented and in-memory, a big shift from traditional databases. Spend time learning about its unique features like data compression, parallel processing, and built-in analytics.

Understanding the nuances of the HANA database is pivotal for ABAP developers making the transition to SAP S/4HANA. Here's how you can dive into the HANA database.

1. Basics of HANA Database: Understand that SAP HANA is a column-oriented, in-memory database designed for high-performance transactions and real-time analytics. It utilizes in-memory storage to reduce the response times for complex queries and allows multi-threaded, parallel processing for high-speed computations.

2. Study the Architecture: SAP HANA's architecture is an integral part that leverages a multitude of technologies like data virtualisation, advanced analytics, databases and application development. Study HANA's multiple engine types, including Row stores, Column Stores, Object Store, and other service components.

3. SQL and SQLScript: HANA database is primarily managed using SQL and SQLScript. While SQL is used for requesting data, SQLScript is SAP's proprietary, procedural extension to SQL that's used for complex and calculation-heavy operations.

4. Data Modeling and Views: Learn about attribute views, analytic views, and calculation views. Understanding these data models will help you make the best use of the HANA database's real-time computing capabilities.

5. Understand Direct Database Access: With the shift from a code-pushdown paradigm to bringing the code to where data resides, you need to understand how to directly access the database and perform operations in the database layer using ABAP Managed Database Procedures (AMDP).

6. Utilize Training Resources: SAP and other online platforms offer excellent resources for learning HANA, including beginner-friendly tutorials, advanced training, and certification courses.

7. Get Hands-on Experience: Nothing beats practical experience. Use a sandbox system if possible, or consider SAP's HANA Express Edition, which is a streamlined version of the HANA platform that can run on a personal computer.

8. Join SAP Community: Be part of online forums and communities. They provide a platform to discuss doubts, stay updated with the latest changes and interact with other HANA enthusiasts or experts.

Remember, diving into HANA database does not mean you need to forget everything you learned as an ABAP developer, instead, it is about extending your existing ABAP skills into a whole new paradigm. With persistence and active learning, you will find that the transition opens up a new world of possibilities in the SAP space.

 

Learn AMDP (ABAP Managed Database Procedures) 

As you delve into S/4HANA, you’ll encounter AMDP, a critical tool that helps developers manage and control database procedures. AMDP lets you leverage the processing power of HANA by bringing code down to the database layer.

ABAP Managed Database Procedures (AMDP) is a new framework in the AS ABAP system, introduced to allow ABAP developers to write database procedures directly within ABAP. In essence, it represents a paradigm shift, blending the power of ABAP with HANA's advanced database capabilities.

Here's what a seasoned ABAP Developer needs to know about AMDPs:

  1. Procedure Definition: AMDP is essentially a class method defined in ABAP, but implemented in SQLScript - the database manipulation language used in SAP HANA.

  2. Multilingual Intent: AMDP allows combining the logic of ABAP and SQLScript within a single method, enabling ABAP developers to leverage SAP HANA's advanced capabilities directly, without requiring extensive knowledge of SQLScript.

  3. Database Layer Processing: By utilising AMDP, ABAP developers can push computation-intensive operations down to the database layer. This helps leverage SAP HANA’s in-memory processing power for optimal performance.

  4. Transactional Behaviour: AMDPs have integrated transactional behaviour. An AMDP method is executed in the same database session and under the same transaction context as the calling ABAP program.

  5. SQLScript Execution: AMDPs serve as a wrapper around SQLScript code and are executed natively on SAP HANA. This means calculations are performed at the database level, resulting in faster data processing and decreased memory usage in the application server.

  6. Underlying Metadata Access: AMDPs provide access to HANA-specific features such as temporary tables, parallel processing, and partitioning of tables which are unavailable in Open SQL.

Remember, AMDPs don't replace standard ABAP or Open SQL, but rather offer an alternative way to interact with the HANA database. They provide an effective way to bridge the gap between ABAP and SAP HANA, allowing developers to take their data manipulation skills to the next level while making the most of HANA's high-performance capabilities.

 

Spotlight on CDS (Core Data Services) Views 

CDS Views are central to S/4HANA and a departure from classic SE11 views. As an ABAP developer, mastering CDS Views is crucial as they provide a seamless way to define semantic data models and the SQL-based data access layer for any application.

Core Data Services (CDS) Views are a significant development in the SAP HANA database, enabling developers to create semantically rich data models to structure the underlying database layer neatly. Here's what you, as a long-time ABAP developer, need to know about them:

1. SQL-Extended Views: CDS views are extensions of the ubiquitous SQL standard. They encapsulate complex SQL queries into reusable views, dramatically simplifying data access and manipulation.

2. Enhanced Data Modelling: Rather than simply acting as windows onto your tables, CDS views are part of a sophisticated data modelling infrastructure that includes features like associations and path expressions. This leads to cleaner, more intuitive data models.

3. Semantic Layer: CDS views provide a semantic layer (metadata and annotations), resulting in a more meaningful representation of data models. This becomes highly beneficial when you deal with business applications requiring specific data interpretations.

4. Data Services and Consumables: CDS views can expose data services for UIs, enable integration with OData services, and be consumed by analytical tools.

5. Built-In Functions: CDS views come with a set of SQL functions, enabling you to perform complex calculations and operations within the database itself rather than passing data back and forth between your application and the database.

6. ABAP Integration: CDS views are integrated into ABAP Dictionary and can be directly used in ABAP programs using standard Open SQL.

7. Authorisation Management: CDS views support object-based and field-based authorisations, offering flexible and robust security options for data access.

Remember, adopting CDS Views does not mean you will abandon your foundational ABAP knowledge. Instead, think of it as an addition to your current skill set that will help you develop more efficient and powerful applications in the S/4HANA landscape.

 

Explore Fiori and UI5 

User experience upgrades are a major aspect of S/4HANA. SAP's proprietary design language, Fiori, and its client-side HTML5 rendering library, UI5, form the foundation of S/4HANA's user interface. Although not directly in the ABAP domain, understanding and being able to customise the new user interface is a valuable skill-set.

SAP Fiori and SAPUI5 are integral components of the user experience design for modern SAP applications. Here's how understanding these aspects can influence you as veteran ABAP Developer:

SAP Fiori: It is a design language and user experience approach developed by SAP for all their applications. Fiori aims to provide a seamless, intuitive user experience across various platforms and devices. It includes a set of design principles and offers a collection of apps with a simple and easy-to-use experience for broadly used SAP software functions that work seamlessly across devices - desktop, tablet, or smartphone.

SAPUI5: SAPUI5 (or SAP user interface for HTML5), on the other hand, is a collection of libraries for web developers to build responsive web applications, used typically for creating Fiori applications.

Impact on ABAP Developers:

1. New Responsibility Spectrum: The advent of Fiori and SAPUI5 does not replace ABAP but extends your responsibility spectrum to front-end and user-experience design too. While you might be heavily rooted in backend development, understanding these front-end concepts can elevate your development work to align with modern UX practices.

2. Shift in Development Approach: Fiori and SAPUI5 demand a shift from a traditional programming approach to embrace a more design-thinking methodology. You need to consider visual appeal, end-user needs, responsiveness, and adaptability of apps on various platforms.

3. Understanding Fiori Architecture: The Fiori architecture comprises the Fiori Launchpad, Fiori client, and ABAP front-end and back-end servers. Understanding this helps you structure and orient your ABAP code to support Fiori applications.

4. Use of Fiori Elements: Fiori Elements provide a framework to create SAP Fiori applications, reducing the amount of front-end coding required and accelerating application development. Your focus would remain on the back-end services and annotations.

5. Impact on Existing SAP Modules: Fiori is a standard part of S/4HANA, and many traditional SAP GUI transactions now have a Fiori counterpart. Over time, a good grasp of Fiori will be essential in maintaining these pieces of functionality.

6. Skills Upscaling: Learning Fiori and SAPUI5 not only helps you remain up-to-date with industry trends but also can provide an edge in your career, making you a comprehensive ABAP developer with both backend and frontend prowess.

In a nutshell, Fiori and SAPUI5 highlight the importance of user experience and interfaces in modern application development. As an ABAP developer, you're invited to be part of this evolution, creating more user-centered, responsive, and visually appealing SAP solutions.

 

Understand S/4HANA’s New Data Model 

S/4HANA has brought in a simplified data model. Traditional aggregate and index tables (like BSEG and BSIS) were eliminated in favour of more efficient real-time calculations. To make the most of these changes, you'll need to adapt your ABAP programming to S/4HANA’s new data model.

SAP S/4HANA's new data model is one of its most pivotal advancements. Here's a high-level perspective on what the new data model means for you as an experienced ABAP developer:

1. Simplification: The guiding principle of the S/4HANA data model is simplification. Many aggregate and index tables used in previous versions of SAP ERP (like Material Ledger or Profitability Analysis tables) have been eliminated. These are replaced by HANA's in-memory capabilities to perform calculations in real time for analytical purposes.

2. Real-Time Processing: The new data model, leveraged by HANA's columnar in-memory database, allows real-time operational analytics directly on the live transactional data instead of depending on aggregate tables or separate analytical systems.

3. Compatibility Views: To ensure compatibility with previous versions, SAP introduced Compatibility views. It means existing ABAP code can still access, for example, the MKPF and MSEG tables - even though they do not physically exist anymore in S/4HANA. This is achieved using view structures that simulate the old tables.

4. New Tables: New tables have been introduced, such as ACDOCA for Universal Journal, MATDOC for Inventory Management, and PRCD_ELEMENTS for Condition Pricing. These tables store data in a more streamlined manner, reducing redundancy.

5. Impact on ABAP Programs: The new data model impacts traditional ABAP programming in several ways. For instance, standard database operations performed on certain tables like BSEG, KONV, etc., in ECC are now replaced by accessing new tables like ACDOCA and PRCD_ELEMENTS in S/4HANA.

6. CDS Views: Core Data Services (CDS) views are an integral part of the new data model, allowing developers to define semantic, reusable data models in the database layer.

7. Embedded Analytics: The simplified data model in conjunction with the power of HANA's in-memory database enables embedded analytics – allowing analytical operations and transactions to be performed in the same system.

The new data model in S/4HANA indicates a shift from traditional database operations to highly optimised operations suited for an in-memory database like HANA. As an ABAP developer, familiarising yourself with this new data model is essential as it is central to the way SAP S/4HANA operates, influencing how future applications will be developed.

 

Upgrade Your Skills with ABAP for HANA 

ABAP on its own isn’t going away, but it does have new capabilities when used in conjunction with HANA. Acquaint yourself with the concepts of ABAP for HANA, which represent an evolution of the ABAP language toward more integration with the in-memory capabilities offered by the HANA database.

ABAP for HANA integrates the power of HANA within ABAP, allowing the development of highly optimised applications for the SAP HANA database. It's not a new language, but an evolved form of ABAP, wherein existing ABAP skills can be utilised to leverage HANA's capabilities.

Here's how you can broaden your skill set for ABAP for HANA:

1. Understand HANA and Its Advantages: Start by comprehending the fundamentals of SAP HANA and its benefits like in-memory computing and columnar storage.

2. SQL and SQLScript: Upgrade your SQL skills, specifically towards SQLScript, the set of SQL extensions optimized for SAP HANA. Understanding features like the parallel processing of commands and the analysis possibilities for data-intensive calculations at the database level can be handy.

3. Code Pushdown Concept: Learn about the code pushdown concept, which is all about moving data-intensive calculations from the application server to the database layer. This optimizes performance by minimizing data transfer and exploiting the computational capabilities of HANA.

4. Core Data Services (CDS): CDS views enhance the capabilities of ordinary database views by enabling you to define complex views which were not possible earlier. Learning how to use CDS views is fundamental to ABAP for HANA development.

5. ABAP Managed Database Procedures (AMDP): AMDP enables the creation of stored procedures directly in ABAP, where you can put real processing load on the HANA database. This can deliver significant performance improvements for your applications.

6. ALV with Integrated Data Access (ALV with IDA): ALV with IDA allows you to display large amounts of data in ALV format using the computing power of SAP HANA. Familiarize yourself with this to exploit SAP HANA's capabilities more effectively.

7. Utilize Training Resources: SAP offers numerous resources and courses on ABAP for SAP HANA. Explore these to extend your knowledge. Also, consider obtaining a relevant certification which likely enhances your credibility.

8. Experiment in a Sandbox Environment: Get hands-on experience. Apply what you learn in a test or sandbox environment. Code review and testing will give you better insight and understanding.

As you dive into ABAP for HANA, remember that it's more about adding new tools and techniques to your repertoire, rather than discarding your existing ABAP knowledge. Embrace the opportunity to learn and grow with the new advancements in SAP technology. By doing so, you'll position yourself well for the future landscape of the SAP ecosystem.

Extensibility in S/4HANA - Keep that Core Pure

SAP S/4HANA extends the capabilities of traditional ERP systems, combining real-time data, intelligent technologies, and an in-memory database. The extensibility approaches for SAP S/4HANA focus on enhancing the business functionalities according to specific needs without disrupting the core standard system.

There are two key extensibility options that ABAP developers should focus on:

In-App Developer Extensibility: This type of extensibility occurs directly within the S/4HANA system. The nature of the changes can range from simple field adaptations to business logic or UI adjustments. The approach primarily involves ABAP Development Tools in Eclipse (ADT) and the main programming model is called ABAP Programming Model for SAP Fiori. This involves business-oriented development of SAP Fiori applications in ABAP. Developers familiar with ABAP can use Core Data Services (CDS), Business Object Processing Framework (BOPF), SADL, and SAP Fiori Elements.

Side-by-Side Extensibility: This is another real-time integration of native cloud applications developed in SAP Cloud Platform using the available services, and incorporating them into the S/4HANA system processes without the risk of modifying the system itself. By utilising open-standard technologies like HTML5, Java, SAP HANA, and XSJS, developers can create tiny, decoupled applications that handle specific business tasks and connect to S/4HANA through APIs.

Extensions can be created in S/4HANA on-premise and S/4HANA cloud, but there are certain restrictions on the cloud version mainly in order to safeguard best performance, maintenance schedules, and to maintain a clean core. It is suggested to use the cloud platform to augment and complement cloud functionality.

Deciding which approach to implement depends on the specific use case, complexity of the requirement, the nature of the business process to be extended, and how deep in the system the extension should reach.

Traditional ABAP developers will find the transition to these extensibility options smoother if they are comfortable with new tools like Eclipse, modern design principles and web development languages. To stay updated, continuous learning and adoption of new skills is essential for ABAP developers in this technology shift.

Knowledge of OData Services, SAP Gateway, SAP UI5, Fiori, and cloud platform services will be instrumental to make extensions in SAP S/4HANA.

SAP Trainings and Certifications 

Don’t overlook the value of formal training in this journey. SAP offers a myriad of courses tailored to enhance your understanding of S/4HANA. Additionally, obtain an HANA certification to validate your skills, which is also a testament to your competency to prospective employers.

Transitioning to S/4HANA from a traditional ABAP background can be streamlined with the right training and certifications. Here are some of the most relevant ones to assist you in this transformation:

1. Trainings:

  • SAP Learning Hub: SAP Learning Hub provides a vast array of courses over various SAP topics, including S/4HANA and ABAP programming for HANA.

 

  • Introduction to Application Programming on SAP S/4HANA (S4DEV): 
    • This course provides participants with an overview of the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP). The goal is to create an SAP Fiori app touching every area needed to develop and deliver this app to the SAP Fiori launchpad.
    • After a short introduction to SAP S/4HANA and SAP Fiori, the first step is to create CDS projection views on top of existing data model views using the ABAP Development Tools (ADT). Exposing the CDS projection views as a business service makes it possible to consume the model data in a SAPUI5 app, which is built using SAP Fiori elements.
    • Using the behavior of a RAP business object, transactional processing is enabled in the app. Determinations, validations and actions are also added through this. As additional functionality draft-support and value helps are implemented. To make the app available in the SAP Fiori launchpad, catalogs with tiles and target mappings are created.

  • SAP HANA - Programming (HA400):

    • This course is designed for developers and consultants with ABAP programming experience who are transitioning to S/4HANA. 

    • In this course, you learn how you can use SAP HANA and new features in the ABAP programming language to optimize existing ABAP code and to develop innovative ABAP applications to enhance your business.
  • SAP HANA 2.0 SPS06 Modeling (HA300): 
    • This course is also available in a self-paced e-learning format with an active subscription to the SAP Learning Hub, as HA300E.
    • This course teaches the core capabilities of SAP HANA for calculation view modeling, and covers mainly graphical modeling, as well as SQL- based modeling (table functions and procedures) with a focus on performance. It also introduces core data services as a flexible method to model a persistence layer.
    • The participants will gain knowledge about the management of modeling content in the SAP Web IDE for SAP HANA, and the key principles of object and data access security in a modeling context.

  • Extensibility for S/4HANA (S4D425):

    • This course covers the different extensibility approaches for SAP S/4HANA that are available to customers and partners:
      • In-app extensibility focuses on adaptations created by key users that have a deep business knowledge but no technical expertise. In-app extensibility comprises adding new fields or custom logic and adapting the standard UIs. Creating Custom UIs, and custom business objects is also supported. In contrast to in-app extensibility, side-by-side extensibility on SAP Cloud Platform allows developers to build their own applications, enhancing and supplementing SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud.
    • In on-premise environments with SAP S/4HANA, apart from the in-app and side-by-side extensibility one has the full flexibility of extensions developed with ABAP code through the ABAP in Eclipse IDE:
      • This comprises enhancement of CDS views, redefinition of Gateway Services etc.

  • Data Modelling in ABAP Dictionary and ABAP Core Data Services (S4D430) : This course extends the skills that you acquired in the Basic and Intermediate ABAP programming courses with the ability to analyze and define data models. You will define database tables in the ABAP dictionary, and views in ABAP core data services (CDS). You will model relations, add SQL logic, and enrich your views with metadata. In the end, you will protect your data against unauthorized read access and learn how to extend foreign data models.

 

  • Transactional Apps with the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (S4D437) This course covers the various aspect of developing transactional apps with the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP): 
    • Defining the data model and the behavior of a RAP Business Object (BO) with ABAP Core Data Services (ABAP CDS)
    • Using the Entity Manipulation Language (EML) to access RAP BOs
    • Implementing the behavior of a RAP BO with a generic data access layer (managed approach) or based on existing (legacy) code (unmanaged approach).
    • Draft-enabling RAP-based applications
    • Developing SAP Fiori apps based on composite RAP BOs

2. Certifications:

  • SAP Certified Development Specialist - ABAP for SAP HANA 2.0 (E_HANAAW_18) : 
    • The "SAP Certified Development Specialist - ABAP for SAP HANA 2.0" certification exam verifies that the candidate has the knowledge of programming ABAP for SAP HANA required by the profile of an SAP ABAP development consultant. This certificate builds on both the basic knowledge gained in related ABAP-for-HANA training and documentation, as well as on practical experience gained as a member of a SAP HANA team, where the candidate would apply this knowledge practically in projects. A strong background in ABAP programming is highly recommended.

  • SAP Certified Associate – Back-End Developer - ABAP Cloud (C_ABAPD_2309) : 

    • The "SAP Certified Associate – Back-End Developer - ABAP Cloud" certification exam validates that you possess ABAP programming language fundamentals based on ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model on SAP Business Technology Platform and principles of building custom extensions in SAP S/4HANA Cloud required of the back-end developer profile. This certification proves that you have an overall understanding and in‐depth skills to participate as a member of project team in a mentored role.

  •  SAP Certified Associate – SAP Fiori Application Developer (C_FIORD_2404) : 

    • This certification verifies that you possess the required skills in the area of SAP Fiori application development as it pertains to the Frontend Developer profile. This certification builds on the basic knowledge gained by a developer from formal SAP LEarning training and preferably refined by practical experience within a SAP Fiori application development project team in a mentored role. This is an entry level certification.

Explore these resources, commit to continuous learning, and progressively upgrade your skills. Also consider being part of the SAP community and relevant forums as it allows networking with peers, sharing knowledge, and staying updated on the latest trends and changes in the SAP landscape. Remember that your journey to mastery in S/4HANA as a veteran ABAP Developer involves not just understanding the new system, but also how your role and the nature of your work evolves along with it.

Making a shift from ABAP to S/4HANA is an exciting journey that presents an opportunity to acquire new skills and advance your SAP career. Yes, it can be challenging, but with right mindset, continuous learning and remaining committed to the process, you can smoothly ride the wave of this technology transformation.

entry.title: How to find CDS views for DB table fields
entry.content:
We were new on our journey to clean core and were struggeling with the access to the well known tables.

How can we get the date fron the CDS views, if we do not know the name of the CDS view name?
We developers an all consultants are very familiar with the names of database tables and fields, as we do it since decades. But how to find the matching CDS view field?

You have some possibilities:

  • Use the Cloudification Repository Viewer 
    for the GitHub Repository for S/4HANA Cloud - Released objects for ATC Check (Cloudification Repository) 

  • Do the ATC check with the Cloudification Repository and hope that a successor object is displayed
    (Not well maintained and still to grow)
  • Have a look at the VDM and hope that you get a glimpse. You must know the technical description for the business object, so it's getting complicated.
    Example you have to know, that the corresponding BO for the db table QMSM is named "Quality Notification Task" and you can find the CDS I_QltyNotificationTask

    But you don't find all and everything....

So I decided to build my own tool.

This consists of two parts
First - Analyse
Analyse once a great amount of CDS views, selected by name and put the results into a database table. An index.

Second - Search and Display
Have a report (old fashioned) which can select for any given CDS view field all database fields or vica versa in a very short time.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Analyser - the heart of it all

Select all the CDS views, which you are interested in. I.e. all Views beginnig with I_ and R_ and P_ or so on.
Let it run in the background and wait some, but not much time.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Search and Display

Just find all the interesting stuff

 

Find the coding of both programs in github

entry.title: Working days between two dates in RAP CDS views
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION

In many business processes customer service agreements are made based on response time in business days. Of course, that gives companies the need to determine when they are required to respond. They also want to know if the business processes are running as required or if they were late with actions. This way they can improve business processes and optimize them.

I have found though that working with working days in SAP RAP wasn’t easy or fast and thus I needed to create a solution.

SAP System

This blog is based on S4CORE Release 106 SP-Level 5

Technical Solution

Determining working day information in SAP RAP not easy. There is a CDS view called FCLM_BAM_DATE3 that is based on the standard configuration in factory calendar table TFACS and it lets you determine which days are working days. It’s neither published though (and with this name, it’s probably never going to be) nor is it fast or efficient in trying to determine the amount of working days between two dates.

Determining the days between two days would be possible using a table function but we wouldn’t be able to filter on it.

A third option would be to create a table that contains just that: A table with a start and end date with the amount of working days in between. That would create a very big table though that would grow exponentially for each date added.

So I decided to do a combination. Reading if a date is a working day using a standard function module and filling a table with the amount of days since the start of the factory calendar. Then calculate the difference in working days between two dates using a CDS view on top of it. This is very fast because per date only 2 selections and a single calculation are needed when building CDS a output.

Table ZWORKINGDAYS

To make sure that determining the number of working days between two dates is fast, I created a table with a single date as a key-field and an accompanying field with the number of working days since the beginning of the calendar.

To determine the number of working days between two days I then only need to read 2 records in the table and determine the difference between the number of working days we read there.

An additional key-field is needed because there can be multiple factory calenders.

@EndUserText.label : 'Workingdays since first day in factory calendar'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zworkingdays {
  key mandt      : mandt not null;
  key ident      : wfcid not null;
  key datum      : datum not null;
  workday_number : int4;
}

Class ZCL_DATE and Report ZFILL_WORKINGDAYS

Of course I need to fill the table. For this we use Function DATE_CHECK_WORKINGDAY which we encapsulated inside class zcl_date. Then I use this in program ZFILL_WORKINGDAYS where I determine the start year and end year of the factory calendar. For all the days in between a record in the table ZWORKINGDAYS is created

The program needs run for every factory calendar and everytime the factory calendar changes.

Method ZCL_DATE->DATE_CHECK_WORKINGDAY

METHODS IS_WORKING_DAY
importing
  value(IDENT) type TFACS-IDENT
returning
  value(RV) type BOOLE_D .
      
METHOD IS_WORKING_DAY.
  "Check if this is a working day. 
  rv = abap_false.

  CALL FUNCTION 'DATE_CHECK_WORKINGDAY'
    EXPORTING
      date                       = me->gv_date
      factory_calendar_id        = IDENT
      message_type               = 'I'
    EXCEPTIONS
      date_after_range           = 1
      date_before_range          = 2
      date_invalid               = 3
      date_no_workingday         = 4
      factory_calendar_not_found = 5
      message_type_invalid       = 6
      OTHERS                     = 7.

  "Only make changes to working days
  IF sy-subrc = 0.
    rv = abap_true.
  ENDIF.

ENDMETHOD.

PROGRAM ZFILL_WORKINGDAYS

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*& Report ZFILL_WORKINGDAYS
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
REPORT zfill_workingdays.

DATA: db_tfacs TYPE tfacs.

SELECTION-SCREEN BEGIN OF BLOCK params WITH FRAME TITLE TEXT-100.
  SELECTION-SCREEN BEGIN OF LINE.
    SELECTION-SCREEN COMMENT 1(22) TEXT-101.
    PARAMETERS p_ident LIKE db_tfacs-ident DEFAULT 'NL'.
  SELECTION-SCREEN END OF LINE.

  PARAMETERS p_del  TYPE boole_d DEFAULT abap_false.
  PARAMETERS p_test TYPE boole_d DEFAULT abap_true.

SELECTION-SCREEN END OF BLOCK params.

"*** DELETE EXISTING ENTRIES IN DB *** *
DELETE FROM zworkingdays WHERE ident = p_ident.
data(deleted_rows) = sy-dbcnt.

IF p_del = abap_false.  " we will insert new entries after we deleted the  existing ones...
  "*** GET FIRST AND LAST DATE IN FACTORY CALENDAR *** *
  SELECT SINGLE
    FROM tfacs
  FIELDS MIN( jahr ) AS jahr
   WHERE ident = @p_ident
    INTO @DATA(first_year).

  SELECT SINGLE
    FROM tfacs
  FIELDS MAX( jahr ) AS jahr
   WHERE ident = @p_ident
    INTO @DATA(last_year).

  DATA(first_date) = CONV datum( |{ first_year }0101| ).
  DATA(last_date)  = CONV datum( |{ last_year  }1231| ).

  "*** GENERATE WORKING DAYS LIST *** *
  DATA: workingdays_t LIKE TABLE OF zworkingdays.

  DATA(counter)       = CONV int4( 0 ).
  DATA(go_start_date) = zcl_date=>instance( first_date ).

  WHILE go_start_date->get_dats( ) LE last_date.
    DATA(is_Working_Day)  = go_start_date->is_working_day( p_ident ).
    counter = counter + COND #( WHEN is_Working_Day = abap_true THEN 1 ).

    APPEND VALUE zworkingdays(
      ident           = p_ident
      datum           = go_start_date->get_dats( )
      workday_number  = counter
    ) TO workingdays_t.

    go_start_date = go_start_date->add_days( 1 ).
  ENDWHILE.

  DATA(count_of_insert) = lines( workingdays_t ).

  "*** UPDATE TABLE *** *
  INSERT zworkingdays FROM TABLE workingdays_t.
  data(inserted_rows) = sy-dbcnt.
ENDIF.  " p_del = abap_false.

"*** COMMIT OR ROLLBACK *** *
WRITE   |Table: ZWORKINGDAYS|.
WRITE / |       Factory Calendar ID: `{ p_ident }`|.
WRITE /.
write / |       { deleted_rows } entries deleted.|.
WRITE / |       { count_of_insert } dates prepared for insertion.|.
WRITE / |       { inserted_rows } entries inserted. |.
WRITE /.

IF p_test = abap_false.
  COMMIT WORK AND WAIT.
  WRITE / |Changes committed.|.
ELSE.
  ROLLBACK WORK.
  WRITE / |Changes rolled back (TEST MODE).|.
ENDIF.

After using the program to fill the table the contents looks like this:

 

Table contents

Every date available in the factory calendar has a amount of days since the start of the factory calendar.

CDS Entity view ZI_WORKINGDAYS

This is a view on top of our table ZWORKINGDAYS. If there is another way to determine the number of working days since the beginning of the factory calendar, this could be replaced.

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Root interface workingdays'

define view entity ZI_WORKINGDAYS as
  select from zworkingdays
{
    key ident      as Ident,
    key datum      as Datum,
    workday_number as WorkdayNumber
}

CDS Entity view ZI_WORKINGSDAYS_BETWEEN

This view calculates the working days between two dates.

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Workingdays between two dates'

define view entity ZI_WORKINGSDAYS_BETWEEN as select from ZI_WORKINGDAYS as wd1
    association[1..*] to ZI_WORKINGDAYS as wd2 on wd2.Ident = wd1.Ident
{
    key wd1.Ident
  , key wd1.Datum as DatumLow
  , key wd2.Datum as DatumHigh
  , wd2.WorkdayNumber - wd1.WorkdayNumber as WorkdaysBetween
  , abs( wd2.WorkdayNumber - wd1.WorkdayNumber ) as WorkdaysBetweenAbs
}

The view uses ZI_WORKINGDAYS twice. Once for determining the number of working days since the start of the factory calendar for the first date and a second time for the second date.The calculation is then between these two dates.

For ease of use I calculate both the the difference between the two days and the absolute difference between these days.

Example how the calculation works

Calculation example

Usage example

Using the CDS view ZI_MATERIAL_RECEIVE_DATE I can now determine the working days since the first time a certain material was received.

@EndUserText.label: 'Date a material was received'
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
define view entity ZI_Material_Receive_Date as select from I_GoodsMovementCube as gmc
    association [1..1] to ZI_Workingdays_Between as _wd
                                                   on _wd.Ident     = 'NL'
                                                  and _wd.DatumLow  = $projection.GoodsReceiptDate
                                                  and _wd.DatumHigh = $session.system_date  
{
    key gmc.Material
  , cast( min(gmc.PostingDate) as mm_a_goods_receipt_date preserving type ) as GoodsReceiptDate
  , _wd
} where gmc.StockChangeCategory = 'GR'
    and (    gmc.GoodsMovementType = '501'
          or gmc.GoodsMovementType = '505' )
